name: Hit Marker
description: A short visual or audio confirmation that an attack connected with a valid target and produced a combat result.
design_notes: Hit markers should be driven by confirmed damage outcomes, not raw collision. They can communicate armor, shield, critical, weak point, kill, or immunity, but too many categories can become noisy. Multiplayer games must distinguish predicted feedback from server-confirmed results.
design_purpose: Confirm successful hits quickly, especially at range or through effects.
Differentiate blocked, armored, critical, weak-point, or kill results.
Support player aim correction without requiring large floating numbers.
player_fantasy: The player feels confident that an attack landed and understands the rough quality of the result.

parameters.name: marker_linger_ms
parameters.description: Display window for the hit marker.

parameters.name: critical_marker_scale
parameters.description: Size multiplier for critical or weak-point hits.

parameters.name: marker_audio_variant_count
parameters.description: Number of sounds used for different hit categories.

parameters.name: multi_hit_merge_window_ms
parameters.description: Window for combining many rapid hits into one feedback event.

parameters.name: predicted_hit_opacity
parameters.description: Opacity for locally predicted markers before confirmation.

edge_cases: Damage over time should not spam hit markers every tick unless the design intentionally wants that feedback.
Stealth, smoke, darkness, or hidden targets may need suppressed markers to avoid revealing information.
Shotguns and area effects need merge rules so audio and markers do not stack excessively.
Network prediction should correct false positives without producing distracting marker pops.
common_bugs: Marker plays on blocked, immune, or friendly hits because any overlap counts.
Critical marker appears for overkill damage rather than weak-point metadata.
Marker sound is mixed too loudly when many pellets hit at once.
Predicted marker remains after the server rejects the hit.
balancing_notes: Strong hit markers improve readability but can reveal targets through smoke, darkness, or cover.
Different marker types should communicate important combat state, not cosmetic complexity.
accessibility_notes: Provide visual and audio confirmation options independently, with color-safe marker shapes.
Allow hit marker size, opacity, and sound volume to be adjusted separately from damage numbers.

implementation_notes.unity: Bind the UI to gameplay state through events or observable view models, not direct polling from presentation widgets; support UI Toolkit, uGUI, and input glyph updates from the active Input System scheme.
implementation_notes.godot: Drive Control nodes from a small view-model dictionary or signal payload, and keep input focus, localization, and scaling separate from gameplay state.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use UMG or CommonUI widgets fed by gameplay events, with focus navigation, platform glyphs, and replicated-state boundaries handled outside widget animation code.
implementation_notes.web: Render the UI from normalized game state, keep input mapping and localization data outside component logic, and avoid making animation completion the source of truth.

example_games.title: Call of Duty 4: Modern Warfare
example_games.note: Uses hit markers to confirm successful shots.

example_games.title: Apex Legends
example_games.note: Uses hit feedback to distinguish shields, flesh damage, and knocks.
